algorithms.title: Suspected Sepsis Field Recognition and Management
algorithms.description: Sequence for recognizing and managing suspected sepsis in the field.
steps: Identify a suspected or known infection source (respiratory, urinary, wound, etc.).
Check temperature — recognize that both fever and hypothermia can indicate sepsis.
Assess for tachycardia, tachypnea, and altered mental status.
Assess perfusion for signs of progression toward septic shock (delayed capillary refill, cool/mottled or warm/flushed skin, hypotension).
If the pattern fits sepsis: provide supportive care (oxygen as needed, IV access and fluids per protocol, cardiac monitoring).
Transport rapidly with early hospital notification, given the time-sensitive nature of sepsis treatment.
Maintain heightened suspicion in older adults, who may present atypically (e.g., confusion without dramatic fever).

algorithms.title: Abdominal/Flank Pain Field Differential Approach
algorithms.description: Sequence for evaluating abdominal or flank pain with attention to dangerous mimics.
steps: Characterize the pain: location, radiation, quality (colicky vs. constant), and associated symptoms (vomiting, hematuria, bowel changes).
Assess vital signs and perfusion status.
If GI bleeding signs present (hematemesis, melena, hematochezia): assess perfusion for hypovolemic shock and treat per protocol.
If bowel obstruction pattern present (colicky pain, distension, vomiting, no BM/flatus): provide supportive care, no oral intake, prompt transport.
If flank pain present: consider renal colic, but evaluate for dangerous mimics — especially AAA in older/vascular-risk patients, and pyelonephritis with systemic infection signs.
Apply the general red-flag framework (pain out of proportion to exam, inability to find a comfortable position, altered mental status) regardless of the presumed diagnosis.
Transport promptly, especially when red flags or dangerous mimics cannot be confidently excluded in the field.
